questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a feature of the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: India follows a Parliamentary form of government, not Presidential. The President is the nominal head while the Prime Minister is the real executive authority.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: The concept of 'Basic Structure' of the Constitution was established in which case?

options.B: Kesavananda Bharati vs State of Kerala
options.C: Minerva Mills vs Union of India
options.D: Maneka Gandhi vs Union of India

questions.explanation: The Basic Structure doctrine was established in Kesavananda Bharati vs State of Kerala (1973). The Supreme Court ruled Parliament cannot alter the basic structure of the Constitution.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: Which Schedule of the Indian Constitution deals with allocation of seats in the Rajya Sabha?
questions.explanation: The Fourth Schedule deals with allocation of seats in the Rajya Sabha to States and Union Territories.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: Which writ is issued by a court to a public official to perform a mandatory duty?
questions.explanation: Mandamus means 'we command'. It is issued to a public authority to perform a mandatory duty. It cannot be issued against a private individual or the President/Governor.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: The 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act is related to:
questions.explanation: The 73rd Amendment (1992) gave constitutional status to Panchayati Raj Institutions, adding Part IX and the Eleventh Schedule with 29 functional items.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: Which Article of the Indian Constitution abolishes untouchability?
questions.explanation: Article 17 abolishes untouchability and forbids its practice in any form. The Protection of Civil Rights Act, 1955 provides penalties for preaching and practicing untouchability.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: The 42nd Amendment Act is also known as:
questions.explanation: The 42nd Amendment Act (1976) is called the 'Mini Constitution' because it made the most comprehensive changes to the Constitution, including adding the words 'Socialist', 'Secular' and 'Integrity' to the Preamble.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: Which body recommends the creation of new All India Services?
questions.explanation: Under Article 312, the Rajya Sabha can recommend the creation of new All India Services by passing a resolution supported by two-thirds of members present and voting.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: The Inter-State Council is established under which Article?
questions.explanation: Article 263 empowers the President to establish an Inter-State Council to investigate and discuss subjects of common interest between the Centre and States.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a Fundamental Duty under Article 51A?
questions.explanation: Paying taxes is a legal obligation but NOT listed among the Fundamental Duties under Article 51A. The 11 Fundamental Duties were added by the 42nd Amendment.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: The concept of Judicial Review in India is based on:

options.D: Both Rule of Law and Procedure Established by Law

questions.explanation: Judicial Review in India is based on both Rule of Law (from British law) and Procedure Established by Law (Article 21). After Maneka Gandhi case, it evolved to include Due Process aspects.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: Which Constitutional body conducts elections to the offices of President and Vice President?
questions.explanation: The Election Commission of India superintends, directs and controls elections to Parliament, State Legislatures, and to the offices of President and Vice President.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: The Panchayat Raj system in India operates at how many levels?
questions.explanation: The Panchayat Raj system operates at three levels: Gram Panchayat (village), Panchayat Samiti (block) and Zilla Parishad (district). This was constitutionalized by the 73rd Amendment.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: Which Article empowers the President to proclaim a Financial Emergency?
questions.explanation: Article 360 empowers the President to proclaim Financial Emergency if he is satisfied that the financial stability or credit of India is threatened. This has never been proclaimed so far.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: The Comptroller and Auditor General of India acts as the friend, philosopher and guide of:
questions.explanation: The CAG acts as the friend, philosopher and guide of the Public Accounts Committee (PAC). The CAG's reports are examined by the PAC and the Committee on Public Undertakings.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: Which commission recommended the concept of 'Union of States' instead of 'Federation of States'?
questions.explanation: The Drafting Committee of the Constituent Assembly, chaired by Dr. B.R. Ambedkar, preferred the term 'Union of States' to emphasize that India's federation is not the result of an agreement among states.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: Money Bills can be introduced only in:
questions.explanation: Under Article 109, Money Bills can be introduced only in the Lok Sabha. After passing, it is sent to Rajya Sabha which must return it within 14 days with or without recommendations.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: The Directive Principles of State Policy are contained in which Part of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: Part IV (Articles 36-51) contains the Directive Principles of State Policy. They are not justiciable but are fundamental in governance. Part III contains Fundamental Rights.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: Which Amendment introduced the Right to Education as a Fundamental Right?
questions.explanation: The 86th Amendment Act (2002) inserted Article 21A making Right to Education a Fundamental Right for children aged 6-14 years. It also modified Article 45 and added a new Fundamental Duty.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: The concept of Public Interest Litigation (PIL) was introduced by Justice:
questions.explanation: The concept of PIL was pioneered by Justice V.R. Krishna Iyer and Justice P.N. Bhagwati in the late 1970s-80s to provide access to justice for the disadvantaged sections of society.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: Which among the following is NOT a qualification to become a member of Rajya Sabha?

options.A: Must be a citizen of India
options.B: Must be at least 30 years of age
options.C: Must possess such other qualifications as prescribed by Parliament
options.D: Must be a resident of the state from which elected

questions.explanation: There is no constitutional requirement for a Rajya Sabha member to be a resident of the state from which they are elected, though the Representation of People Act, 2003 added this requirement.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: The National Commission for Scheduled Castes derives its powers from which Article?
questions.explanation: Article 338 provides for the National Commission for Scheduled Castes to investigate and monitor safeguards provided for SCs, inquire into complaints, and make recommendations.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: Which of the following is true about the Attorney General of India?

options.A: He is appointed by the Prime Minister
options.B: He can participate in Parliament proceedings but cannot vote
options.C: He holds office during the pleasure of the Lok Sabha Speaker
options.D: He must be a retired Supreme Court judge

questions.explanation: The Attorney General is appointed by the President, holds office during the President's pleasure, and has the right to participate in proceedings of both Houses of Parliament but cannot vote.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: The 44th Constitutional Amendment restored the right to:

options.C: Life and Personal Liberty from arbitrary action

questions.explanation: The 44th Amendment (1978) revived the protection of Articles 20 and 21 by providing that these rights cannot be suspended even during a National Emergency. It also removed Right to Property as a Fundamental Right.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: Which committee recommended giving constitutional status to Gram Sabha?
questions.explanation: The L.M. Singhvi Committee (1986) recommended that Gram Sabha should be given constitutional status. The 73rd Amendment later gave constitutional recognition to Gram Sabha.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: Under which Article can the President of India refer any question of law or fact to the Supreme Court for its opinion?
questions.explanation: Article 143 empowers the President to consult the Supreme Court on any question of law or fact. The Court's opinion is advisory and not binding on the President.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: The concept of 'Collective Responsibility' of the Council of Ministers is enshrined in:
questions.explanation: Article 75(3) provides that the Council of Ministers shall be collectively responsible to the House of the People (Lok Sabha). If a no-confidence motion is passed, the entire ministry falls.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: Which one of the following is NOT a part of the electoral college for the election of the President?

options.C: Elected members of State Legislative Assemblies

questions.explanation: Under Article 54, the electoral college for the President consists of elected members of both Houses of Parliament and elected members of State Legislative Assemblies. Nominated members are excluded.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: The First Law Commission of independent India was chaired by:
questions.explanation: The First Law Commission of independent India was established in 1955 under the chairmanship of M.C. Setalvad, the first Attorney General of India.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: Which Schedule was added by the 73rd Amendment to the Constitution?
questions.explanation: The 73rd Amendment (1992) added the Eleventh Schedule containing 29 functional items for Panchayats. The 74th Amendment added the Twelfth Schedule with 18 functional items for Municipalities.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: The maximum strength of Lok Sabha as fixed by the Constitution is:
questions.explanation: The maximum strength of Lok Sabha is fixed at 552 — up to 530 from States, up to 20 from Union Territories, and 2 nominated Anglo-Indian members (though the 104th Amendment removed this provision).
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: Which Article of the Constitution deals with the Finance Commission?
questions.explanation: Article 280 provides for the constitution of a Finance Commission by the President every five years to recommend distribution of net proceeds of taxes between Centre and States.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: The concept of 'Secular' was added to the Preamble by which Amendment?
questions.explanation: The word 'Secular' along with 'Socialist' and 'Integrity' was added to the Preamble by the 42nd Amendment Act, 1976 during the Emergency period.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: Which of the following writs means 'to have the body'?
questions.explanation: Habeas Corpus literally means 'to have the body'. It is issued to produce a detained person before the court to examine the legality of detention. It is the most important writ for personal liberty.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: The National Human Rights Commission was established in which year?
questions.explanation: NHRC was established on 12 October 1993 under the Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993. It is a statutory body that investigates violations of human rights.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: Under Article 356, President's Rule in a State can be imposed for a maximum period of:
questions.explanation: President's Rule under Article 356 can be imposed initially for six months and can be extended up to a maximum of three years with parliamentary approval every six months.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: Which body has the power to approve the proclamation of emergency issued by the President?
questions.explanation: A proclamation of emergency under Article 352 must be approved by both Houses of Parliament by a special majority within one month to continue in force.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: The idea of a Constituent Assembly for India was first put forward by:
questions.explanation: The idea of a Constituent Assembly for India was first put forward by M.N. Roy, a pioneer of communist movement in India, in 1934. It was later adopted by the Indian National Congress in 1935.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: Which of the following Fundamental Rights is available only to citizens and not to foreigners?
questions.explanation: Article 19 (six freedoms including speech, assembly, movement etc.) is available only to Indian citizens. Articles 14, 21, and 23 are available to all persons including foreigners.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: The Sarkaria Commission was set up to review:

options.A: Financial relations between Centre and States

questions.explanation: The Sarkaria Commission was established in 1983 under Justice R.S. Sarkaria to examine and review Centre-State relations. It submitted its report in 1988 with 247 recommendations.
questions.subject: polity

questions.question: The Battle of Plassey (1757) was fought between:
questions.explanation: The Battle of Plassey was fought on June 23, 1757, between the British East India Company led by Robert Clive and the Nawab of Bengal, Siraj-ud-Daulah.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: Who founded the Brahmo Samaj?
questions.explanation: Brahmo Samaj was founded by Raja Ram Mohan Roy in 1828 in Calcutta. It was a monotheistic reformist movement aimed at fighting social evils.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: The Quit India Movement was launched in which year?
questions.explanation: The Quit India Movement was launched by Mahatma Gandhi on August 8, 1942, at the Bombay session of the All India Congress Committee with the call 'Do or Die'.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: Which of the following Vedas is the oldest?
questions.explanation: The Rig Veda is the oldest of the four Vedas, composed approximately between 1500-1200 BCE. It consists of 1,028 hymns organized into 10 books.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: The Dandi March was associated with:
questions.explanation: The Dandi March (Salt March) was a 241-mile march led by Gandhi from March 12 to April 6, 1930, marking the beginning of the Civil Disobedience Movement.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: The Indus Valley Civilization was primarily located in:
questions.explanation: The Indus Valley Civilization (3300-1300 BCE) was primarily located in the northwestern Indian subcontinent along the Indus and Ghaggar-Hakra rivers, covering present-day Pakistan and northwest India.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: Who was the founder of the Maurya Empire?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya founded the Maurya Empire around 321 BCE with the guidance of his mentor Chanakya (Kautilya). He overthrew the Nanda dynasty.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: The Revolt of 1857 started from:
questions.explanation: The Revolt of 1857 started on May 10, 1857, at Meerut when sepoys of the 3rd Bengal Light Cavalry refused to use the new Enfield rifle cartridges.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: The Gupta period is often referred to as the:
questions.explanation: The Gupta period (320-550 CE) is called the Golden Age of India due to remarkable achievements in science, mathematics, art, literature, astronomy and philosophy.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: Who among the following introduced the Doctrine of Lapse?
questions.explanation: Lord Dalhousie introduced the Doctrine of Lapse (1848-56) whereby any princely state under British suzerainty would be annexed if the ruler died without a male heir.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: The Champaran Satyagraha (1917) was related to:
questions.explanation: The Champaran Satyagraha was Gandhi's first major civil disobedience in India, fighting for indigo plantation workers exploited by European planters under the tinkathia system in Bihar.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: Which Mughal emperor built the Taj Mahal?
questions.explanation: Shah Jahan built the Taj Mahal (1632-1653) in memory of his wife Mumtaz Mahal in Agra. It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of the New Seven Wonders of the World.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: The Indian National Congress was founded in which year?
questions.explanation: The Indian National Congress was founded on 28 December 1885 by A.O. Hume, along with Dadabhai Naoroji and Dinshaw Wacha. The first session was held in Bombay.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: Who was the last Mughal Emperor?
questions.explanation: Bahadur Shah Zafar (1837-1857) was the last Mughal Emperor. He was made the symbolic leader of the 1857 revolt and was later exiled to Rangoon (Yangon) by the British.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: The Rowlatt Act was passed in which year?
questions.explanation: The Rowlatt Act was passed on March 10, 1919, allowing the government to imprison any person suspected of terrorism without trial for up to two years. It led to widespread protests.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: Which ancient university is considered the first residential university in the world?
questions.explanation: Nalanda University (5th century CE) is considered the first residential university in the world. It attracted scholars from all over Asia and had about 10,000 students and 2,000 teachers.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: The Jallianwala Bagh massacre took place on:
questions.explanation: The Jallianwala Bagh massacre occurred on April 13, 1919, when General Dyer ordered his troops to fire on a peaceful gathering in Amritsar, killing hundreds of civilians.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: Who wrote 'Arthashastra'?
questions.explanation: Arthashastra was written by Kautilya (Chanakya), the chief minister of Chandragupta Maurya. It is a treatise on statecraft, economic policy and military strategy.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: The Non-Cooperation Movement was launched in:
questions.explanation: The Non-Cooperation Movement was launched by Gandhi in 1920, calling for boycott of British goods, institutions, and honors. It was suspended after the Chauri Chaura incident in 1922.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: Who among the following was the first woman President of the Indian National Congress?
questions.explanation: Annie Besant became the first woman President of the Indian National Congress in 1917 at the Calcutta session. Sarojini Naidu was the first Indian woman to hold that position (1925).
questions.subject: history

questions.question: The Bhakti Movement in medieval India was initiated by:
questions.explanation: While Shankaracharya pioneered Advaita Vedanta, the Bhakti Movement as a devotional mass movement was significantly shaped by Ramanuja (11th-12th century) who advocated Vishishtadvaita philosophy.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: The Battle of Panipat was fought in which year for the first time?
questions.explanation: The First Battle of Panipat (April 21, 1526) was fought between Babur and Ibrahim Lodi. Babur's victory established the Mughal Empire in India.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: Who is known as the 'Grand Old Man of India'?
questions.explanation: Dadabhai Naoroji is called the 'Grand Old Man of India'. He was the first Indian to be elected to the British Parliament and propounded the 'Drain of Wealth' theory.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: The Harappan site of Lothal is located in:
questions.explanation: Lothal is located in Gujarat near the Gulf of Khambat. It was an important port city of the Indus Valley Civilization known for its ancient dockyard, bead factory and fire altars.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: Who started the Home Rule Movement in India?

options.A: Annie Besant and Bal Gangadhar Tilak

questions.explanation: The Home Rule Movement was started in 1916 by Annie Besant (All India Home Rule League) and Bal Gangadhar Tilak (Indian Home Rule League) demanding self-governance for India.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: The ancient Indian text 'Meghadutam' was written by:
questions.explanation: Meghadutam (The Cloud Messenger) is a Sanskrit lyric poem written by Kalidasa, often considered the greatest poet and dramatist of the Gupta period.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: The Swadeshi Movement was launched in response to:
questions.explanation: The Swadeshi Movement was launched in 1905 in response to Lord Curzon's decision to partition Bengal. It promoted Indian goods and boycotted British products.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: Which dynasty built the Khajuraho temples?
questions.explanation: The Khajuraho temples were built by the Chandela dynasty between 950-1050 CE in Madhya Pradesh. They are famous for their nagara-style architecture and erotic sculptures.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: The Cabinet Mission Plan was proposed in:
questions.explanation: The Cabinet Mission of 1946 proposed a three-tier federal structure for India: provinces, groups of provinces, and the Union. It rejected the demand for a separate Pakistan.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: Who gave the slogan 'Jai Hind'?
questions.explanation: Subhas Chandra Bose popularized the slogan 'Jai Hind' (Victory to India) as the battle cry of the Indian National Army (Azad Hind Fauj) during World War II.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: The Vijayanagara Empire was founded by:
questions.explanation: The Vijayanagara Empire was founded in 1336 by brothers Harihara I and Bukka Raya I of the Sangama dynasty, with the guidance of sage Vidyaranya. Hampi was its capital.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: The treaty of Srirangapatna was signed between Tipu Sultan and:
questions.explanation: The Treaty of Srirangapatna (1792) was signed between Tipu Sultan of Mysore and Lord Cornwallis after the Third Anglo-Mysore War. Tipu had to cede half his territory and pay war indemnity.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: Who among the following was the leader of the Bardoli Satyagraha?
questions.explanation: The Bardoli Satyagraha (1928) was led by Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel against the increase in land revenue in Bardoli taluka, Gujarat. Its success earned Patel the title 'Sardar'.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: The Iron Pillar at Delhi belongs to which period?
questions.explanation: The Iron Pillar at Mehrauli, Delhi, dates to the Gupta period (circa 402 CE). It is renowned for its rust-resistant composition and is attributed to Chandragupta II.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: Who wrote 'Discovery of India'?
questions.explanation: Discovery of India was written by Jawaharlal Nehru during his imprisonment at Ahmednagar Fort (1942-46). It covers Indian history from the Indus Valley Civilization to independence.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: The Permanent Settlement was introduced by:
questions.explanation: The Permanent Settlement (1793) was introduced by Lord Cornwallis in Bengal. It fixed the land revenue to be paid by zamindars permanently, creating a landed aristocracy.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: Which Chola ruler is credited with a naval expedition to Southeast Asia?
questions.explanation: Rajendra Chola I (1014-1044 CE) launched a naval expedition against the Srivijaya Empire in Southeast Asia, successfully conquering parts of modern-day Malaysia and Indonesia.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: The Simon Commission was boycotted because:
questions.explanation: The Simon Commission (1928) was boycotted because it had no Indian members. All seven members were British parliamentarians, which was seen as an insult to Indian self-governance aspirations.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: Who established the Arya Samaj?
questions.explanation: Arya Samaj was founded by Swami Dayananda Saraswati in 1875 in Bombay. It promoted the authority of the Vedas, opposed idol worship, and worked for social reform.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: The Indian Independence Act was passed by the British Parliament on:
questions.explanation: The Indian Independence Act was passed on July 18, 1947, receiving Royal Assent the same day. It provided for the creation of two independent dominions: India and Pakistan, effective August 15, 1947.
questions.subject: history

questions.question: The Tropic of Cancer passes through how many Indian states?
questions.explanation: The Tropic of Cancer passes through 8 Indian states: Gujarat, Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, West Bengal, Tripura, and Mizoram.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: Which is the longest river in India?
questions.explanation: The Ganga is the longest river flowing entirely within India at about 2,525 km. It originates from Gangotri glacier and flows into the Bay of Bengal.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: The Western Ghats are also known as:
questions.explanation: The Western Ghats, also known as Sahyadri, run parallel to the western coast for about 1,600 km. They are a UNESCO World Heritage Site and a biodiversity hotspot.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: The correct order of soil types from north to south in India is:
questions.explanation: The general distribution follows: Alluvial soils (Indo-Gangetic plains), Black soils (Deccan Plateau), Red soils (Southern Plateau), and Laterite soils (extreme south).
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: The 'Doldrums' is a zone of:
questions.explanation: The Doldrums (ITCZ) is a belt of low atmospheric pressure near the equator characterized by calm or light variable winds due to intense solar heating.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: Which Indian state has the longest coastline?
questions.explanation: Gujarat has the longest coastline of about 1,600 km among Indian states, bordering the Arabian Sea and the Gulf of Kutch and Gulf of Khambhat.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: The Chilika Lake is located in which state?
questions.explanation: Chilika Lake is located in Odisha and is the largest coastal lagoon in India and the second largest in the world. It is a Ramsar site and important bird habitat.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: Which plateau is known as the 'Roof of the World'?
questions.explanation: The Tibetan Plateau is known as the 'Roof of the World'. It is the world's highest and largest plateau with an average elevation of about 4,500 meters above sea level.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: The monsoon winds in India are caused primarily by:

options.B: Differential heating of land and sea

questions.explanation: Indian monsoons are primarily caused by differential heating of land and sea, creating pressure differences. The ITCZ shift, jet streams, and Somali current also play important roles.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: Which of the following rivers does NOT originate from Western Ghats?
questions.explanation: The Narmada originates from Amarkantak in Madhya Pradesh (Vindhya Range), not from the Western Ghats. Godavari, Krishna, and Kaveri all originate from the Western Ghats.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: The Siachen Glacier is located in:
questions.explanation: The Siachen Glacier, the longest glacier in the Karakoram Range, is located near the India-Pakistan-China border. It is about 76 km long and the second longest glacier outside the polar regions.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: Which type of climate does Cherrapunji experience?
questions.explanation: Cherrapunji (Sohra) in Meghalaya experiences tropical wet climate. It is one of the wettest places on Earth, receiving extremely heavy rainfall due to orographic effect on monsoon winds.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: The Deccan Plateau is bounded by which mountain range on the north?
questions.explanation: The Deccan Plateau is bounded on the north by the Vindhya and Satpura ranges, on the east by the Eastern Ghats, and on the west by the Western Ghats.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: Which strait separates India from Sri Lanka?
questions.explanation: The Palk Strait separates India from Sri Lanka. It is about 53-80 km wide and connects the Bay of Bengal with the Palk Bay and Gulf of Mannar.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: Ring of Fire is associated with:
questions.explanation: The Ring of Fire is a major area of volcanic and seismic activity encircling the Pacific Ocean basin, home to about 75% of the world's active volcanoes and 90% of earthquakes.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: The Thar Desert is mainly located in which Indian state?
questions.explanation: The Thar Desert (Great Indian Desert) is primarily located in Rajasthan (about 61% of the desert), extending into Gujarat, Haryana, and Punjab. It covers about 200,000 sq km.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: Which pass connects Jammu & Kashmir with Ladakh?
questions.explanation: Zoji La Pass (3,528 m) on National Highway 1D connects the Kashmir Valley with Ladakh. It is a crucial passage on the route from Srinagar to Leh.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: Coriolis force causes winds to deflect to the:

options.C: Right in Northern, Left in Southern Hemisphere
options.D: Left in Northern, Right in Southern Hemisphere

questions.explanation: Due to Earth's rotation, the Coriolis force deflects winds to the right in the Northern Hemisphere and to the left in the Southern Hemisphere. This affects wind patterns, ocean currents and cyclone rotation.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: The highest peak of the Eastern Ghats is:
questions.explanation: Jindhagada Peak (also called Arma Konda) at 1,680 m in Andhra Pradesh is the highest peak of the Eastern Ghats. Anai Mudi is the highest peak in the Western Ghats.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: Which ocean current warms the western coast of Europe?
questions.explanation: The Gulf Stream and its extension, the North Atlantic Drift, carry warm water from the Gulf of Mexico to the western coast of Europe, making winters milder than at similar latitudes elsewhere.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: The Sunderban Delta is formed by which rivers?
questions.explanation: The Sundarbans Delta, the world's largest mangrove forest, is formed by the confluence of the Ganga and Brahmaputra rivers in the Bay of Bengal, spanning India and Bangladesh.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: What is the standard meridian of India?
questions.explanation: The standard meridian of India is 82°30' E longitude, passing through Mirzapur in Uttar Pradesh. Indian Standard Time (IST) is 5 hours 30 minutes ahead of GMT.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: Which type of vegetation is found in the Andaman and Nicobar Islands?
questions.explanation: The Andaman and Nicobar Islands have tropical evergreen forests due to heavy rainfall and warm temperature throughout the year. They also have mangrove and coastal vegetation.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: The Brahmaputra river is known as _____ in Tibet:
questions.explanation: The Brahmaputra is known as Yarlung Tsangpo in Tibet, Dihang/Siang in Arunachal Pradesh, and Jamuna in Bangladesh. It is one of the largest rivers by discharge.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: Which Indian city has the highest population density?
questions.explanation: Mumbai has one of the highest population densities among Indian cities, with over 20,000 people per sq km in the municipality area. This is due to the island geography limiting expansion.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: Black soil in India is primarily found in:
questions.explanation: Black soil (Regur soil) is found in the Deccan Trap region, formed from volcanic basaltic rocks. It is rich in iron, lime, and alumina and ideal for cotton cultivation.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: Which earthquake zone is most of the Himalayan region classified under?
questions.explanation: Most of the Himalayan region falls under Seismic Zone V, the highest risk zone in India. The Himalayan belt is seismically active due to the collision of Indian and Eurasian tectonic plates.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: Which country shares the longest land border with India?
questions.explanation: Bangladesh shares the longest land border with India at about 4,096 km. China comes second at about 3,488 km, followed by Pakistan at about 3,323 km.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: The Kalahari Desert is located in:
questions.explanation: The Kalahari Desert spans Botswana, Namibia, and South Africa in Southern Africa. It covers about 900,000 sq km and is a semi-arid sandy savanna.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: Which of the following is a cold ocean current?
questions.explanation: The Humboldt Current (Peru Current) is a cold ocean current flowing along the western coast of South America. Cold currents lead to arid conditions along the coast.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: Sundarbans is famous for:
questions.explanation: The Sundarbans is the world's largest mangrove forest, covering about 10,000 sq km across India and Bangladesh. It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and home to the Royal Bengal Tiger.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: Which dam is built on the Narmada River?
questions.explanation: Sardar Sarovar Dam is built on the Narmada River in Gujarat. It is one of the largest dams in the world and a major source of irrigation and power for Gujarat.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: What is a 'Rift Valley'?

options.B: Valley formed by tectonic faulting and subsidence

questions.explanation: A rift valley is formed by tectonic faulting when the Earth's crust is pulled apart, and the central block sinks. The Great Rift Valley in East Africa is the most famous example.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: Contour lines on a topographical map represent:
questions.explanation: Contour lines join points of equal elevation above sea level. Closely spaced contour lines indicate steep slopes, while widely spaced lines indicate gentle slopes.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: Which type of rainfall is caused by mountains?
questions.explanation: Orographic rainfall occurs when moist air is forced to rise over mountains, cooling and condensing to form clouds and precipitation. The windward side receives more rain than the leeward side.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: The Peninsular Plateau of India is part of:
questions.explanation: The Peninsular Plateau of India is a part of the ancient Gondwanaland, which included present-day South America, Africa, Australia, and Antarctica. It is one of the oldest landmasses.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: Which Indian state is the largest producer of tea?
questions.explanation: Assam is the largest tea-producing state in India, contributing about 52% of India's total tea production. India is the world's second-largest producer of tea after China.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: El Niño is associated with:
questions.explanation: El Niño is the warming of sea surface temperatures in the central and eastern tropical Pacific Ocean. It affects global weather patterns, often causing drought in India and Australia.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: The northernmost point of India is:
questions.explanation: Indira Col in Siachen Glacier (Jammu & Kashmir) is the northernmost point of India at approximately 35°40' N latitude. Kanyakumari is the southernmost point of mainland India.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: Which layer of the atmosphere contains the ozone layer?
questions.explanation: The ozone layer is located in the stratosphere, approximately 15-35 km above Earth's surface. It absorbs most of the Sun's harmful ultraviolet radiation, protecting life on Earth.
questions.subject: geography

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a function of the Reserve Bank of India?
questions.explanation: Collecting direct taxes is a function of CBDT under the Ministry of Finance, not the RBI. RBI's functions include issuing currency, regulating banks, and managing forex reserves.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: GDP at factor cost equals:

options.A: GDP at market price + Subsidies - Indirect Taxes
options.B: GDP at market price - Subsidies + Indirect Taxes
options.C: GDP at market price - Indirect Taxes + Subsidies
options.D: GDP at market price + Indirect Taxes - Subsidies

questions.explanation: GDP at factor cost = GDP at market price - Indirect Taxes + Subsidies. Factor cost represents actual production costs without fiscal distortion.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: Which Five Year Plan adopted 'Growth with Social Justice and Equity'?
questions.explanation: The Ninth Five Year Plan (1997-2002) adopted 'Growth with Social Justice and Equity' as its objective, targeting 7% GDP growth with emphasis on agriculture and rural development.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: Which of the following is a direct tax?
questions.explanation: Income Tax is a direct tax levied directly on individuals and entities. GST, Custom Duty, and Excise Duty are indirect taxes where the burden can be passed to consumers.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: Fiscal deficit in a government budget is:

options.B: Total expenditure - Total receipts excluding borrowings

questions.explanation: Fiscal Deficit = Total Expenditure - Total Receipts (excluding borrowings). It indicates the government's total borrowing requirements.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: NITI Aayog replaced which institution?
questions.explanation: NITI Aayog (National Institution for Transforming India) was established on January 1, 2015, replacing the Planning Commission. It serves as a think tank for the government.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: The Goods and Services Tax (GST) was implemented in India from:
questions.explanation: GST was implemented on July 1, 2017, through the 101st Constitutional Amendment Act. It subsumed multiple indirect taxes into a single unified tax system.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a component of the current account of Balance of Payments?
questions.explanation: FDI falls under the Capital Account, not the Current Account. The Current Account includes trade in goods and services, income, and current transfers (remittances).
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: Priority Sector Lending norms require banks to lend what percentage to priority sectors?
questions.explanation: RBI mandates that domestic commercial banks must lend 40% of Adjusted Net Bank Credit to priority sectors including agriculture, micro enterprises, education, and housing.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: What does CRR stand for in banking?
questions.explanation: Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR) is the percentage of total deposits that banks must keep as reserves with the RBI. It is a monetary policy tool to control money supply.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: The base year for calculating India's current GDP series is:
questions.explanation: The current GDP series uses 2011-12 as the base year. The CSO (now NSO) shifted to the new base year in January 2015, also adopting the GVA methodology.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: MUDRA Bank was established for:
questions.explanation: MUDRA (Micro Units Development and Refinance Agency) Bank was launched in 2015 to provide funding to micro and small enterprises through schemes like Shishu, Kishore, and Tarun.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: Which of the following is the regulator of the Indian stock market?
questions.explanation: SEBI (Securities and Exchange Board of India) is the regulatory body for the securities market in India. It was established in 1988 and given statutory powers in 1992.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: What is 'Repo Rate'?

options.A: Rate at which RBI borrows from commercial banks
options.B: Rate at which banks lend to each other
options.C: Rate at which RBI lends to commercial banks

questions.explanation: Repo Rate is the rate at which the RBI lends short-term money to commercial banks against government securities. It is a key tool for controlling inflation.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: The Laffer Curve shows the relationship between:
questions.explanation: The Laffer Curve illustrates the relationship between tax rates and tax revenue. It suggests that beyond a certain point, higher tax rates actually decrease revenue due to disincentive effects.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: Which sector contributes the most to India's GDP currently?
questions.explanation: The services sector contributes about 54% of India's GDP. Agriculture contributes roughly 15% and industry about 31%. The services-led growth is a distinctive feature of India's economy.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: The concept of 'Demographic Dividend' refers to:
questions.explanation: Demographic dividend refers to the economic growth potential from a favorable shift in age structure where the working-age population (15-64) is larger than the dependent population.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: Which organization publishes the Human Development Index (HDI)?
questions.explanation: The Human Development Index is published by UNDP in its annual Human Development Report. HDI measures average achievements in health (life expectancy), education, and standard of living (GNI per capita).
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: Inflation indexed bonds protect investors against:
questions.explanation: Inflation indexed bonds provide returns that are adjusted for inflation, protecting investors' purchasing power. The principal or coupon payments increase with the Consumer Price Index.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: The National Food Security Act was enacted in which year?
questions.explanation: The National Food Security Act, 2013 aims to provide subsidized food grains to approximately two-thirds of India's population. It covers up to 75% of rural and 50% of urban population.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: Special Economic Zones (SEZs) in India are governed by:
questions.explanation: The Special Economic Zones Act was enacted in 2005 to provide a consistent policy framework for SEZs. SEZs offer tax incentives, duty-free imports, and simplified regulations.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: What is the primary objective of the 'Make in India' initiative?

options.B: Transforming India into a global manufacturing hub

questions.explanation: 'Make in India' was launched in September 2014 to transform India into a global manufacturing hub, encourage foreign investment, and create jobs in 25 key sectors.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: The Phillips Curve shows the relationship between:
questions.explanation: The Phillips Curve depicts an inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment. As unemployment decreases, inflation tends to increase and vice versa (in the short run).
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: Which committee recommended the introduction of GST in India?
questions.explanation: Multiple committees contributed: the Kelkar Task Force (2003) first proposed GST; the Empowered Committee of State Finance Ministers worked on design; and various others refined the concept.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: The Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (IBC) was enacted in:
questions.explanation: The Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code was enacted in 2016 to consolidate insolvency laws in India. It established NCLT and IBBI to oversee insolvency resolution in a time-bound manner.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: Which scheme provides minimum guaranteed employment of 100 days per year to rural households?
questions.explanation: MGNREGA (Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act, 2005) guarantees 100 days of wage employment per year to every rural household whose adult members volunteer for unskilled manual work.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: What does SLR stand for in banking terminology?
questions.explanation: Statutory Liquidity Ratio (SLR) is the minimum percentage of deposits that a bank must maintain in the form of gold, cash, or approved securities before providing credit.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: The concept of 'Inclusive Growth' means:

options.B: Growth equally shared across all sections of society

questions.explanation: Inclusive growth means economic growth that creates opportunities for all segments of the population and distributes dividends of increased prosperity equitably. It was the theme of the 11th and 12th Five Year Plans.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: Which body regulates the insurance sector in India?
questions.explanation: IRDAI (Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India) regulates and promotes the insurance and re-insurance industry in India. It was established under the IRDA Act, 1999.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: Demonetization of Rs. 500 and Rs. 1000 notes was announced on:
questions.explanation: On November 8, 2016, the Government of India announced demonetization of Rs. 500 and Rs. 1000 banknotes, aiming to curb black money, counterfeit currency, and terrorism financing.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: What is 'Stagflation'?

options.B: High inflation with stagnant economic growth

questions.explanation: Stagflation is an economic condition combining stagnant economic growth, high unemployment, and high inflation simultaneously. It defies the traditional Phillips Curve relationship.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: NABARD was established in:
questions.explanation: NABARD (National Bank for Agriculture and Rural Development) was established on 12 July 1982 as an apex development bank for facilitating credit flow for agriculture and rural development.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: The twin balance sheet problem refers to:

options.C: Current account and capital account deficit

questions.explanation: The twin balance sheet problem refers to the stress on balance sheets of both commercial banks (due to high NPAs) and corporates (due to over-leverage), hampering credit growth and investment.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: Which tax replaced multiple indirect taxes in India?
questions.explanation: GST (Goods and Services Tax) replaced multiple indirect taxes including Central Excise, Service Tax, VAT, CST, Entry Tax, and others, creating 'One Nation, One Tax' system.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: The primary role of commercial banks in credit creation is through:
questions.explanation: Commercial banks create credit through fractional reserve banking. They keep a fraction of deposits as reserves and lend out the rest, multiplying the money supply in the economy.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: Which index measures wholesale price inflation in India?
questions.explanation: The Wholesale Price Index (WPI) measures changes in prices at the wholesale level. It is published weekly by the Office of Economic Adviser in the Ministry of Commerce.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA) replaced:
questions.explanation: FEMA (1999) replaced FERA (Foreign Exchange Regulation Act, 1973). FEMA is more liberal, treating forex violations as civil offenses rather than criminal, facilitating external trade.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: Which of the following is a measure of economic inequality?
questions.explanation: The Gini Coefficient measures income or wealth inequality on a scale of 0 to 1, where 0 represents perfect equality and 1 represents perfect inequality.
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: Public debt of the central government includes:
questions.explanation: Public debt of the central government includes both internal debt (market loans, treasury bills, special securities) and external debt (borrowings from foreign governments and multilateral institutions).
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) of RBI has how many members?
questions.explanation: The MPC has 6 members: 3 from RBI (including the Governor who chairs it) and 3 external members appointed by the Government. It decides the policy interest rate (repo rate).
questions.subject: economy

questions.question: ISRO's Chandrayaan missions are primarily aimed at:
questions.explanation: Chandrayaan missions by ISRO are lunar exploration missions. Chandrayaan-3 (2023) successfully achieved a soft landing near the Moon's south pole.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: CRISPR-Cas9 technology is used for:
questions.explanation: CRISPR-Cas9 is a revolutionary gene-editing technology allowing precise DNA modification. It has applications in treating genetic diseases, agriculture, and biotechnology.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: Which vitamin deficiency causes night blindness?
questions.explanation: Vitamin A (Retinol) deficiency causes night blindness. Vitamin A is essential for rhodopsin production in the retina for low-light vision.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: 5G technology operates primarily in which frequency band?
questions.explanation: 5G operates across multiple bands: Sub-1 GHz (low), 1-6 GHz (mid), and above 24 GHz (mmWave). Mid-band and mmWave are primary 5G frequency ranges.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: Blockchain technology was originally devised for:
questions.explanation: Blockchain was conceived in 2008 by Satoshi Nakamoto for Bitcoin. It serves as a distributed ledger. Applications have since expanded to various fields.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: Which planet in our solar system has the most moons?
questions.explanation: Saturn has the most known moons with over 140 confirmed moons as of 2023, surpassing Jupiter which has about 95 known moons.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: DNA stands for:
questions.explanation: DNA stands for Deoxyribonucleic Acid. It is the hereditary material in humans and almost all organisms, carrying genetic instructions for development and functioning.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: The Newton (N) is the SI unit of force, named after Sir Isaac Newton. One Newton equals the force needed to accelerate 1 kg mass at 1 m/s².
questions.subject: science

questions.question: Which gas is most abundant in Earth's atmosphere?
questions.explanation: Nitrogen makes up about 78% of Earth's atmosphere, followed by oxygen at about 21%, argon at about 0.93%, and carbon dioxide at about 0.04%.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: Photosynthesis primarily takes place in which part of the plant?
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis primarily occurs in leaves, specifically in chloroplasts containing chlorophyll. The process converts CO2 and water into glucose and oxygen using sunlight.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: The Higgs Boson particle is also known as:
questions.explanation: The Higgs Boson, discovered in 2012 at CERN, is nicknamed the 'God Particle'. It gives mass to other fundamental particles through the Higgs field.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: Which Indian satellite navigation system provides regional coverage?
questions.explanation: NavIC (Navigation with Indian Constellation), formerly IRNSS, is India's regional satellite navigation system developed by ISRO. It provides coverage over India and surrounding regions.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of ozone?
questions.explanation: Ozone has the chemical formula O3 (three oxygen atoms). It is found in the stratosphere where it absorbs harmful UV radiation, and at ground level where it acts as a pollutant.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: Antibiotics are effective against:
questions.explanation: Antibiotics are effective against bacteria, not viruses. Using antibiotics for viral infections contributes to antibiotic resistance. Antiviral drugs are needed for viral infections.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: Which Indian mission studied the Sun's corona?
questions.explanation: Aditya-L1, launched in September 2023, is India's first space mission to study the Sun. It orbits at the L1 Lagrange point to observe the Sun's corona and solar wind.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: Which subatomic particle has no charge?
questions.explanation: The neutron is an electrically neutral subatomic particle found in the nucleus. Protons are positively charged, electrons are negatively charged, and positrons are positively charged.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: Artificial Intelligence (AI) was first coined as a term in:
questions.explanation: The term 'Artificial Intelligence' was coined by John McCarthy in 1956 at the Dartmouth Conference, considered the founding event of AI as a field.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: What is the primary function of hemoglobin?
questions.explanation: Hemoglobin is a protein in red blood cells that binds oxygen in the lungs and transports it to tissues throughout the body. It also carries CO2 back to the lungs.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: Nuclear fission involves:
questions.explanation: Nuclear fission is the splitting of heavy atomic nuclei (like Uranium-235 or Plutonium-239) into lighter nuclei, releasing enormous energy. It powers nuclear reactors and atomic bombs.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: Which technology is used in MRI scans?

options.C: Strong magnetic fields and radio waves

questions.explanation: MRI uses strong magnetic fields and radiofrequency pulses to create detailed images of organs and tissues. Unlike X-rays and CT scans, MRI does not use ionizing radiation.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: What is the speed of light in vacuum?
questions.explanation: The speed of light in vacuum is approximately 3 × 10⁸ meters per second (299,792,458 m/s). It is a fundamental constant in physics, denoted by 'c'.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: Which of the following is a renewable source of energy?
questions.explanation: Solar energy is renewable as it is continuously available from the Sun. Coal, natural gas, and petroleum are fossil fuels that take millions of years to form and are non-renewable.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: ISRO's Mars Orbiter Mission (Mangalyaan) was launched in:
questions.explanation: Mangalyaan was launched on November 5, 2013, and entered Mars orbit on September 24, 2014. India became the first nation to succeed in its first Mars mission.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: What is quantum computing based on?

options.B: Quantum bits (qubits) that can be 0, 1, or both

questions.explanation: Quantum computing uses qubits that can exist in superposition (both 0 and 1 simultaneously), enabling exponentially faster computation for certain problems compared to classical computers.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: The element used in pencil leads is:
questions.explanation: Pencil 'leads' are made of graphite (a form of carbon) mixed with clay. Despite the name, they have never contained lead. More graphite makes softer, darker lines.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: Which disease is caused by the deficiency of iodine?
questions.explanation: Iodine deficiency causes goitre (enlarged thyroid gland). Iodine is essential for thyroid hormone production. India's salt iodization program addresses this widespread deficiency.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: LIGO is associated with the detection of:
questions.explanation: LIGO (Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory) detected gravitational waves for the first time in 2015, confirming Einstein's prediction from General Relativity. India plans LIGO-India.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: The pH value of pure water at 25°C is:
questions.explanation: Pure water has a pH of 7 at 25°C, which is neutral. Values below 7 are acidic and above 7 are basic/alkaline. pH is a logarithmic scale of hydrogen ion concentration.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: Which organ in the human body produces insulin?
questions.explanation: Insulin is produced by beta cells in the Islets of Langerhans in the pancreas. Insulin regulates blood glucose levels. Its deficiency or resistance causes diabetes mellitus.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: 3D printing is also known as:
questions.explanation: 3D printing is called additive manufacturing because it creates objects by adding material layer by layer, as opposed to subtractive manufacturing which removes material from a block.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: Which ray is used in remote controls?
questions.explanation: Remote controls use infrared (IR) rays to communicate with devices. IR rays are invisible electromagnetic radiation with wavelengths longer than visible light.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: The mRNA vaccines for COVID-19 work by:

options.B: Injecting genetic instructions to produce spike protein

questions.explanation: mRNA vaccines (like Pfizer, Moderna) deliver genetic instructions for cells to produce the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein, triggering an immune response without causing infection.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: Superconductivity occurs at:
questions.explanation: Superconductivity is a phenomenon where electrical resistance drops to zero at extremely low temperatures. Most conventional superconductors require temperatures near absolute zero (-273.15°C).
questions.subject: science

questions.question: Which satellite of India provides weather forecasting data?
questions.explanation: INSAT (Indian National Satellite System) satellites provide weather forecasting, communication, and broadcasting services. They carry payloads for meteorological observations.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: The process of converting seawater to freshwater is called:
questions.explanation: Desalination is the process of removing salt and minerals from seawater to produce freshwater. Methods include reverse osmosis, thermal distillation, and electrodialysis.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: Which metal is liquid at room temperature?
questions.explanation: Mercury is the only metal that is liquid at room temperature (melting point -38.83°C). It is used in thermometers and barometers but is toxic and an environmental pollutant.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: What does LiDAR technology measure?
questions.explanation: LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) uses laser light to measure distances and create 3D maps. It is used in autonomous vehicles, topography, archaeology, and urban planning.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: The National Supercomputer Mission aims to:
questions.explanation: India's National Supercomputer Mission (launched 2015) aims to build a network of over 70 high-performance computing facilities across academic and research institutions using indigenous technology.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: Which blood group is known as the universal donor?
questions.explanation: O- (O negative) is the universal donor as it can be given to any blood type. O- red blood cells have no A, B, or Rh antigens on their surface.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: The Large Hadron Collider (LHC) is located at:
questions.explanation: The LHC is located at CERN on the Swiss-French border near Geneva. It is the world's largest and most powerful particle accelerator, with a 27 km circumference tunnel.
questions.subject: science

questions.question: The Ramsar Convention is related to conservation of:
questions.explanation: The Ramsar Convention (1971) is an international treaty for conservation and sustainable use of wetlands. India has 75+ Ramsar sites.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: Which greenhouse gas has the highest Global Warming Potential (GWP)?
questions.explanation: Sulfur Hexafluoride (SF6) has the highest GWP of ~23,500 over 100 years. CO2=1, Methane=28-36, N2O=265-298.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: The National Green Tribunal was established under which Act?
questions.explanation: NGT was established on October 18, 2010 under the NGT Act, 2010. It handles cases relating to environmental protection and conservation.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: Which biodiversity hotspot in India is richest in species diversity?
questions.explanation: The Western Ghats is one of world's eight 'hottest hotspots' with 7,402+ flowering plant species, 508 bird species, and 179 amphibian species.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: The Paris Agreement aims to limit global warming to:

options.D: Well below 2°C, pursuing efforts to limit to 1.5°C

questions.explanation: The Paris Agreement (2015) aims to limit warming to well below 2°C, pursuing efforts to limit to 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: The Kyoto Protocol is associated with:
questions.explanation: The Kyoto Protocol (1997) committed industrialized nations to reduce greenhouse gas emissions. It introduced mechanisms like CDM, JI, and emissions trading.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: Which Act provides for the protection of wild animals and birds in India?
questions.explanation: The Wildlife Protection Act, 1972 provides for protection of wild animals, birds, and plants. It established National Parks, Wildlife Sanctuaries, and schedules for protected species.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: The Montreal Protocol deals with:
questions.explanation: The Montreal Protocol (1987) is an international treaty to protect the ozone layer by phasing out production of ozone-depleting substances like CFCs and HCFCs.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: Coral bleaching is caused primarily by:
questions.explanation: Coral bleaching occurs when corals expel symbiotic algae (zooxanthellae) due to stress from rising sea temperatures. Climate change is the primary driver of mass bleaching events.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: The IUCN Red List category 'Critically Endangered' means:

options.A: Species is extinct in the wild
options.B: Species faces extremely high risk of extinction

questions.explanation: IUCN's 'Critically Endangered' category means a species faces an extremely high risk of extinction in the wild. It is just one step below 'Extinct in the Wild'.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: Which of the following is a primary air pollutant?
questions.explanation: SO2 is a primary pollutant emitted directly from sources like fossil fuel combustion. Ozone, PAN, and acid rain are secondary pollutants formed by chemical reactions in the atmosphere.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: Project Tiger was launched in India in which year?
questions.explanation: Project Tiger was launched on April 1, 1973 at Jim Corbett National Park. It aims to ensure the survival and maintenance of the Bengal Tiger population in India.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: The Chipko Movement originated in which state?
questions.explanation: The Chipko Movement originated in 1973 in Chamoli district of Uttarakhand (then UP). Villagers, led by Sunderlal Bahuguna and others, hugged trees to prevent deforestation.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: Bioaccumulation refers to:

options.B: Accumulation of substances in organisms over time

questions.explanation: Bioaccumulation is the gradual accumulation of substances like pesticides or heavy metals in an organism. Biomagnification is the increase in concentration up the food chain.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: Which international agreement addresses trade in endangered species?
questions.explanation: CITES (Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species) regulates international trade in specimens of wild animals and plants to ensure it doesn't threaten their survival.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: What is the primary cause of ocean acidification?
questions.explanation: Ocean acidification is caused by the ocean absorbing excess atmospheric CO2, forming carbonic acid. This lowers ocean pH, threatening marine organisms with calcium carbonate shells.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: The National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC) includes how many missions?
questions.explanation: NAPCC launched in 2008 includes 8 national missions: Solar, Enhanced Energy Efficiency, Sustainable Habitat, Water, Sustaining Himalayan Ecosystem, Green India, Sustainable Agriculture, and Strategic Knowledge.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: Which type of forest covers the maximum area in India?
questions.explanation: Tropical Deciduous forests (Monsoon forests) cover the maximum area in India, found in regions with 70-200 cm rainfall. They shed leaves in the dry season.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: The concept of 'Carbon Footprint' measures:

options.B: Total greenhouse gas emissions caused by an entity

questions.explanation: Carbon footprint measures the total amount of greenhouse gases (expressed in CO2 equivalents) generated by human activities, including transportation, energy use, and consumption.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: Which biosphere reserve in India was the first to be included in the UNESCO MAB network?
questions.explanation: Nilgiri Biosphere Reserve was the first Indian biosphere reserve to be included in the UNESCO Man and Biosphere (MAB) Programme in 2000. It was established in 1986.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: E-waste (electronic waste) primarily contains:

options.B: Toxic metals like lead, mercury, and cadmium

questions.explanation: E-waste contains toxic metals like lead, mercury, cadmium, and chromium along with plastics and glass. Improper disposal causes soil and water contamination and health hazards.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: The 'Green Revolution' in India primarily affected which crop?
questions.explanation: The Green Revolution (1960s-70s) primarily impacted wheat and rice production through high-yielding varieties, irrigation, and chemical fertilizers, making India self-sufficient in food grains.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: Eutrophication in water bodies is caused by:
questions.explanation: Eutrophication occurs when excess nutrients (nitrogen, phosphorus) from agricultural runoff and sewage cause algal blooms, depleting oxygen and killing aquatic life.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: Which gas is released from paddy fields and contributes to global warming?
questions.explanation: Methane (CH4) is released from paddy fields due to anaerobic decomposition in waterlogged conditions. Rice paddies are a significant source of methane, a potent greenhouse gas.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: The Convention on Biological Diversity was signed at:
questions.explanation: The Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) was opened for signature at the Rio Earth Summit in 1992. It has three objectives: conservation, sustainable use, and equitable benefit sharing.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: What is a 'Carbon Sink'?

options.B: A natural or artificial reservoir that absorbs CO2

questions.explanation: A carbon sink is a natural or artificial reservoir that absorbs and stores CO2 from the atmosphere. Forests, oceans, and soil are major natural carbon sinks.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: Which animal is the national aquatic animal of India?
questions.explanation: The Gangetic River Dolphin (Platanista gangetica) was declared India's national aquatic animal in 2009. It is an endangered species found in the Ganges and Brahmaputra river systems.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: Acid rain is primarily caused by emissions of:
questions.explanation: Acid rain is caused by sulfur dioxide (SO2) and nitrogen oxides (NOx) emissions, mainly from fossil fuel combustion. These react with water in the atmosphere to form sulfuric and nitric acids.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: The Biological Diversity Act of India was enacted in:
questions.explanation: The Biological Diversity Act was enacted in 2002 to implement India's obligations under the Convention on Biological Diversity. It established the National Biodiversity Authority.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: Which zone of the ocean receives no sunlight?
questions.explanation: The aphotic zone extends from below 200m depth where no sunlight penetrates. The euphotic zone (0-200m) receives sunlight, and the dysphotic zone receives dim light.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: Minamata disease is caused by:
questions.explanation: Minamata disease is caused by mercury (methylmercury) poisoning. It was first discovered in Minamata, Japan in 1956 due to industrial wastewater discharge into the bay.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: The term 'Biodiversity' was coined by:
questions.explanation: The term 'biodiversity' (short for biological diversity) was coined by Walter G. Rosen in 1985 while organizing a forum. E.O. Wilson popularized it through his writings.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: Which is the most abundant renewable energy source in India?
questions.explanation: Solar energy has the highest potential in India with about 748 GW potential. India receives about 5,000 trillion kWh of solar energy per year due to its tropical location.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: The Namami Gange Programme was launched to:

options.B: Rejuvenate and clean the river Ganga

questions.explanation: Namami Gange was launched in 2014 as a flagship programme for effective abatement of pollution, conservation, and rejuvenation of the Ganga river with a budget of Rs 20,000 crore.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: Which protocol replaced the Kyoto Protocol for post-2020 climate action?
questions.explanation: The Paris Agreement (2015) replaced the Kyoto Protocol for the post-2020 period. Unlike Kyoto, it requires all nations to submit Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs).
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: Biogas primarily consists of:
questions.explanation: Biogas primarily consists of methane (50-75%) and carbon dioxide (25-50%), with traces of hydrogen sulfide, nitrogen, and hydrogen. It is produced by anaerobic decomposition of organic matter.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: The concept of 'Ecological Footprint' was developed by:
questions.explanation: The Ecological Footprint concept was developed by Mathis Wackernagel and William Rees in the 1990s. It measures human demand on nature versus the biosphere's regenerative capacity.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: Which country emits the most greenhouse gases?
questions.explanation: China is currently the world's largest emitter of greenhouse gases, accounting for about 27% of global emissions. The USA is second. However, per capita emissions are higher in the USA.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: The Silent Valley National Park is famous for:
questions.explanation: Silent Valley National Park in Kerala is famous for its pristine tropical rainforests, one of the last undisturbed tracts of South Western Ghats rainforest. The Save Silent Valley movement prevented its destruction.
questions.subject: environment

questions.question: COP stands for in climate change negotiations:
questions.explanation: COP stands for Conference of Parties, the decision-making body of the UNFCCC. COP meetings are held annually where nations negotiate climate action commitments.
questions.subject: environment
